title: Chapter 7: The Dreamer's Sketch
content: I arrive at his door with my armor on.

It is not metal or leather — it is worse. It is clinical detachment, drawn tight as a corset stay around every nerve I possess. My toolkit is organized with surgical precision: chalk, pins, tape measure, shears, marking wheel. Each tool a mediator. Each tool a promise. I will not touch his skin. I will not let my fingers find the hollow of his throat or the architecture of his clavicle the way they did — the way I cannot stop remembering they did — five days ago.
I have rehearsed this. Measure, mark, pin, leave.

Rowan opens the door himself. No assistant, no housekeeper. Just him in a ribbed undershirt the color of undyed wool, the fabric worn soft at the collar, clinging to the architecture of his shoulders. His hair is damp — he's showered recently — and he smells like cedar soap and something underneath it that I refuse to catalog.
"Miss De Vere," he says.
"Mr. Ashworth."
He steps aside. I enter the townhouse without meeting his eyes.

The fitting room is the same parlor where I first measured him, but he's rearranged the furniture. The fitting block sits in the center now, near the tall windows where late afternoon light falls in long amber rectangles. The coat frames hang along the far wall like empty sculptures. My half-constructed work waits on the dress form — the basted shell of his midnight coat, pinned and waiting.
He sits on the block. I set my toolkit on the side table. The chalk clicks against the wood as I lay it out, and the sound is too loud in the quiet room.
I begin.

The undershirt is fine merino — I can feel its texture through my chalk when I run the line along his shoulder seam. Wool against cotton against the warmth of him underneath. I keep my touch light, professional, mediated by the tools I've brought between us like a moat.
"Arms up," I say.
He raises them. I snake the tape measure around his chest, reading the number without looking at his face. The measurement hasn't changed. I mark it anyway.

Snap — I pull the chalk line taut and release it against the fabric. The sharp sound cuts the silence, and I see his jaw shift slightly at the noise. I'm grateful for it. The sound fills the space where conversation should be.
I pin the shoulder seam on the basted coat, my fingers working the fabric, adjusting the ease. I'm steady. I am a professional. I am the continent of ice I have made of myself—
"You're different today," he says.
I slide another pin. "I'm focused."
"No." His voice is quiet but precise — a scalpel, not a sword. "You're careful. There's a difference."
My fingers fumble. A pin slips, and I catch it before it falls, but the motion is clumsy, human, exactly the kind of thing my armor was supposed to prevent. I re-pin the seam without responding.

He watches me work. Not passively — he watches the way a raptor watches a field mouse, with the patience of something that has all the time in the world and knows exactly where its quarry will break cover. His attention is a physical thing, a weight on my shoulders, the back of my neck, the wrists I'm keeping carefully angled away from him.
I should ask him to look away. I don't.

The fitting continues. I adjust the side panels, check the hang of the sleeves, mark the button placement with my chalk wheel. Each touch mediated. Each breath measured. I am a machine of linen and precision and I will not think about the way his pulse beats visibly at the hollow of his wrist, where his arm rests against his knee.
"Can I ask you something?" he says.
"You can ask." I keep my eyes on the basting stitch I'm adjusting at his side seam.

"At the auction — when you first saw the coat. You recognized it before I told you anything about it. You knew its construction method, its era, its likely maker." He pauses. I feel him turning toward me even though I'm not looking. "But you didn't ask about its provenance. You didn't ask where I found it or who it belonged to. You asked about the fabric."
"I'm a tailor. Fabric is my material."
"You asked about the fabric the way a biographer asks about a subject's childhood. Like you already knew the rest of the story and just needed to confirm the beginning."
I keep pinning. My hands are steady. My heart is hammering.
"Where did you study, Orlando?"
The question lands like a stone in still water. He's never used my first name before. The ripple spreads through me, disturbing silt I've kept buried for centuries.
"I'm self-taught," I say.

"Self-taught people have gaps." His voice carries conviction — the certainty of someone who has studied gaps, who perhaps has them himself. "You don't have gaps. You have the opposite of gaps — you have centuries of muscle memory compressed into thirty years of life."
My hands stop moving.

The silence that follows is not empty. It is full — full of the hum rising in my chest, full of the dust-moted light and the distant sound of a taxi on the street below, full of the terrible precision of his observation. He's not accusing. There's no suspicion in his voice, no demand for explanation. He's simply observing the way he observes everything — with that forensic, unhurried attention that sees past surfaces to the structures beneath.
He shouldn't be able to see that. No one sees that.
"Centuries," I repeat, and my voice comes out flatter than I intend. "That's a curious word to choose."
"Is it?" He tilts his head. Not defensive. Genuinely curious — like he's tasting the word alongside me and finding it stranger than he expected.

I resume pinning. The motion is mechanical now, my fingers following paths they've carved into muscle memory over lifetimes I cannot explain to this man sitting on a fitting block in a wool undershirt in his rented townhouse in Manhattan in the year two thousand and twenty-six, watching me with eyes that see too much and understand too little.
"Never mind," he says, and reaches into his back pocket.

He pulls out a moleskine notebook. The black cover is soft and worn at the edges, the elastic band stretched and replaced with a rubber band that has lost its elasticity. It is clearly, obviously, painfully personal — the kind of object a person carries so long it becomes an external organ.
"I want to show you something," he says. "I've never shown anyone this."
He opens the notebook and turns it toward me.

The smell hits me first — old paper, the vanilla-sweet chemical off-gassing of acidic pulp, ink dried to a faintly metallic dust. The scent of a document that has been handled for years, the oils of a single person's fingers worked into every page.
Then I see the sketches.

Dozens of them. On page after page, a single face, drawn and redrawn — from memory, from dreams, from some deep well of recollection that has no business existing in the mind of a man who has never seen this face in waking life. The lines vary in confidence: some are rough, almost frantic, like he was trying to capture the image before it dissolved in the lag between sleep and consciousness. Others are achingly precise — the sweep of a brow, the exact angle of a jaw, the deep classical eyes that I have seen in mirrors across centuries.
The hair is messy and short. The expression shifts from sketch to sketch — guarded, curious, laughing, closed — but the bones beneath are always the same.
They are my bones.
I am looking at myself, drawn by a man who has never seen me, from dreams he's had every night since he was a child.

"I've been drawing this face since I was fourteen," Rowan says. His voice is careful in a different way than mine has been — not the careful of armor, but the careful of someone laying down something fragile and watching to see if it will be stepped on. "I don't know who it is. I've never met them. But I dream about them every night."

My mouth goes dry. Desert-dry, sandpaper throat, the taste of chalk dust and something metallic underneath it — fear, or recognition, or the bitter residue of being seen so completely by someone who doesn't yet understand what he's seeing.

The hum in my chest spikes — not a warning, not the low-voltage alert of danger. This is something else entirely. This is the sound my body makes when a lock turns and a door opens onto a room I didn't know existed. It is the sound of recognition so total it borders on grief.

I should deny it. Every survival instinct I've honed across four hundred years is screaming the same instruction: say that's not me, close the notebook, gather your toolkit, leave. The exit is twelve steps away. I have fled harder situations. I have walked out of burning cities, out of revolutions, out of the arms of people who loved me in forms I no longer wear.
I say: "You said 'them.' Not 'her.'"

Rowan blinks. He looks down at the sketch, then up at me, then back at the sketch — and I watch the moment land, watch the micro-expression of confusion ripple across his face as he processes what he said, what he meant, what his sleeping mind knows that his waking mind refuses to articulate.

"I don't know why," he says slowly. "The face changes sometimes. The bone structure stays the same but..." He trails off. His brow furrows, and I can see him circling something — some vast unnamed shape in the dark water of his own unconscious, rising toward the surface but not yet breaking through. "The gender doesn't."

I am standing in a townhouse in Manhattan in the twenty-first century, and a man I barely know is describing the central mechanism of my existence — the shifting, the fluidity, the way I have been man and woman and both and neither across centuries — and he doesn't know it. He's a sonar pulse bouncing off the hull of a ship he can't see, returning data he can't interpret. But the data is accurate. God help me, the data is flawless.

He's still talking. "It's the same person. I know it's the same person. The eyes are the same, the—" He gestures vaguely at his own face, searching for words. "The architecture is the same. But sometimes it's—"
He stops.

I close the moleskine. My fingers are trembling, just slightly, and I press them flat against the worn black cover to still them before he can notice. The paper beneath the cover stock shifts and whispers. I hand the notebook back to him.
"Finish the sentence," I say.
He looks at me — really looks, the way he looked at the sketches, with that forensic patience that sees structures instead of surfaces. "What sentence?"
"The one you almost said. About the face changing."

The silence stretches between us like a held breath. The light through the windows has shifted — the amber rectangles have crept across the floor, and one of them falls across Rowan's hands where they hold the moleskine. Dust motes turn slowly in the beam.

Rowan opens his mouth. Closes it. The muscles in his jaw work, and I can see him wrestling something — not resistance, exactly, but the peculiar discomfort of a man about to say something that doesn't fit the shape of the world as he understands it.
He opens his mouth again.
"Sometimes..." He pauses. Swallows. Meets my eyes with an expression that is equal parts confession and bewilderment. "Sometimes it's a man."

The word hangs in the air between us. Man. Such a small word. Such a catastrophic word, when spoken by someone who has been dreaming your face across every form you've worn for four centuries and has finally, without knowing it, named the thing you are.

I should correct him. I should redirect, deflect, protect the architecture of secrecy that has kept me alive through plague and purge and the slow grinding entropy of time. That is what I do. That is who I have learned to be.

But I am standing in the amber light of a Manhattan afternoon, and a man in a wool undershirt has just shown me the most intimate map of his unconscious mind, and drawn on that map is every face I have ever worn, and he does not know it, and the not-knowing is the most unbearable thing I have ever witnessed because it means his soul has been searching for me longer than his mind has been alive.

My hum has not stopped. It is resonating in my sternum like a tuning fork struck against the anvil of the impossible. I don't know what my face is doing. I don't know if my armor is still in place or if it has melted like ice under the heat of being recognized by a dreamer who doesn't know he's dreaming true.
"Orlando," he says quietly.
I don't answer.

He's looking at me the way he looked at the coat, at the auction — seeing the construction beneath the surface, the centuries layered into the seams. He doesn't have the framework to understand what he's seeing. Not yet. But he's standing at the edge of it, peering down into a depth he can't measure, and the terrifying thing — the thing that makes my hum spike and my hands shake and my carefully constructed professional armor crack along lines I didn't know were weak — is that he isn't afraid of the fall.
He's compelled by it.
I pick up my chalk. I mark the next button placement. My fingers are not steady, but they are moving, and movement is survival, and I have survived worse than this.
I have survived worse than being seen.
I'm just not sure I've survived better.
